sections.java: // Concatenation, String.format, StringBuilder String name = "Ada"; int count = 3; String a = "Hi " + name + ", x" + count; String b = String.format( "Hi %s, x%d", name, count);
sections.python: # f-strings: expression interpolation inline name = "Ada" count = 3 a = f"Hi {name}, x{count}" # Any expression works inside the braces: b = f"Hi {name.upper()}, x{count * 2}"

sections.title: f-strings Do It All
sections.content: Prefix a string with f and any {expression} inside is evaluated and inserted — variables, method calls, arithmetic. It replaces concatenation, String.format, and MessageFormat in one readable syntax. Bonus: f"{count=}" prints count=3 (name and value) — a debugging gift with no Java equivalent.

sections.java: // Common String operations s.trim(); s.toUpperCase(); s.split(","); String.join(", ", list); s.startsWith("http"); s.contains("://");
sections.python: # Same ideas, snake_case names s.strip() s.upper() s.split(",") ", ".join(items) # join is a str method! s.startswith("http") "://" in s # in, not .contains()

sections.title: Strings Are Immutable — Don't += in a Loop
sections.content: Like Java, Python str is immutable, so result += piece inside a loop builds a new string each iteration — O(n²), the same trap that makes you reach for StringBuilder. The Pythonic StringBuilder is "".join(parts): collect pieces in a list, then join once. Note join is a method on the separator (", ".join(items)), which surprises every Java dev.

sections.java: // Format specifiers via String.format String.format("%.2f", 3.14159); // 3.14 String.format("%,d", 1000000); // 1,000,000 String.format("%10s", "hi"); // right-align
sections.python: # Format spec lives after a colon in the brace f"{3.14159:.2f}" # 3.14 f"{1000000:,}" # 1,000,000 f"{'hi':>10}" # right-align width 10 f"{0.75:.1%}" # 75.0% (percent)

sections.question: What is the Pythonic equivalent of building a String with StringBuilder in a loop?
options: Use += on a str inside the loop — it's optimized
Collect pieces in a list and call separator.join(list) once
Use the StringBuilder class from the stdlib
sections.explanation: Python strings are immutable, so += in a loop is O(n²) — the same reason you use StringBuilder in Java. The idiom is to append pieces to a list and then "".join(pieces) (or any separator) a single time at the end.
